nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; aggregations around isolated coral heads and rubble patches, typically at depths of 10–58 m (recorded range 3–152 m). Juveniles also inhabit harbors and silty rocky reefs.

diet.notes: In the wild, primarily feeds on zooplankton drifting around aggregations, with opportunistic predation on very small fish. In captivity, generally accepts frozen mysis shrimp, enriched frozen brine shrimp, copepods, and high-quality pellet/flake formulations. Multiple small feedings per day (3–5 times) are commonly advised; like most anthias, individuals that go off food can decline rapidly. A refugium producing live copepods and amphipods is helpful for supplemental live prey.

avoidWith: fish that compete aggressively for food

summary: A striking Indo-Pacific anthias marked by a vertical red band that bisects the body, set against warm pink and orange tones. Generally considered reef-safe and is typically kept as a haremic shoal with one dominant male and several females.
careNotes:
The Red Belted Anthias (Pseudanthias rubrizonatus) is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite — all individuals are born female, and the dominant female in a group can transition to male when the existing male is removed. In the wild, they form aggregations around isolated coral heads and rubble patches, subdivided into harems of one dominant male and multiple females; aquarists typically replicate this structure with a single male and 4 or more females. Two males kept together tend to fight intensely.

This species tends to be semi-aggressive, particularly toward conspecifics and other anthias species competing for the same niche. It is generally considered reef-safe with no documented harm to corals or sessile invertebrates.

Like other anthias, individuals are constant-grazing planktivores in the wild and benefit from 3–5 small feedings per day in captivity; an established refugium producing copepods and amphipods is highly beneficial. A tightly-sealed lid is strongly advised — anthias are known jumpers and a high escape risk.

Reported in the Indo-Pacific from the Andaman Sea east to Fiji and the Solomon Islands, north to southern Japan and south to northwestern Australia and the Great Barrier Reef. Typical depth in the wild is 10–58 m (recorded as deep as 152 m), and juveniles are often found in harbors and silty rocky reefs.

Cross-source notes: LiveAquaria states an adult size of about 12.7 cm (5 in) and a 125-gallon (~475 L) minimum tank; FishBase lists a maximum length of 12 cm TL and a preferred temperature range of 24.2–28.9°C. The temperature range used (23–27°C) is averaged across LiveAquaria (22–26°C) and FishBase. Minimum tank volume is set to 500 L (rounded up from 475 L) to provide adequate swimming space for a haremic group of a 12 cm semi-aggressive shoaling species.
breedingNotes: Rarely if ever bred in captivity. As a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, sex change from the dominant female to male can occur in aquaria when the male is removed. No established captive-breeding protocol is documented.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source: water parameters, tank-size minimum, temperament, diet, reef-safe status, color description, and harem grouping.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name (Randall, 1983), taxonomy (Anthiadidae, Perciformes), maximum length (12 cm TL), depth range (10–133 m; typically 10–58 m), and Indo-Pacific distribution. Listed preferred temperature 24.2–28.9°C — averaged with LiveAquaria's 22–26°C to give 23–27°C in the entry.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Tertiary check (taxonomy fallback only): confirmed family Anthiadidae, depth range 3–152 m, protogynous sex change, and harem social structure (one dominant male, multiple females, dominant female transitions to male if male disappears).

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely if ever bred in captivity. Protogynous sequential hermaphrodite; dominant female transitions to male when the group's male is absent. No established captive-breeding protocol documented.
